terms.definition: An acute neurological event caused by infarction or haemorrhage in the brain, leading to focal deficits.
terms.exampleSentence: Following her stroke, she presented with right-sided hemiparesis and expressive dysphasia.
terms.contextNotes: OT input typically begins in the acute stroke unit.

terms.definition: Medical term for a stroke; an interruption of cerebral blood supply causing neurological dysfunction.
terms.exampleSentence: She was admitted to the acute stroke unit following a left middle cerebral artery cerebrovascular accident.
terms.contextNotes: Often abbreviated CVA in handovers.

terms.definition: A progressive neurodegenerative dementia characterised by amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles and memory decline.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient with Alzheimer's disease required cueing for sequencing dressing tasks.
terms.contextNotes: Most common form of dementia in older adults.

terms.definition: A dementia caused by reduced cerebral blood flow, often following multiple small strokes, with stepwise decline.
terms.exampleSentence: Vascular dementia presented with stepwise decline and prominent executive dysfunction.
terms.contextNotes: Often coexists with Alzheimer's disease as mixed dementia.

terms.definition: A dementia featuring fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations and parkinsonism, with cortical Lewy bodies on pathology.
terms.exampleSentence: Lewy body dementia was suspected given the visual hallucinations and fluctuating attention.
terms.contextNotes: Antipsychotic sensitivity is a key safety consideration.

terms.definition: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder marked by tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia and postural instability.
terms.exampleSentence: OT input for Parkinson's disease focused on handwriting, ADL pacing and freezing-of-gait cues.
terms.contextNotes: Symptoms fluctuate with levodopa on/off cycles.

terms.definition: A neurodevelopmental motor coordination disorder affecting planning and execution of movement, also called DCD.
terms.exampleSentence: The child with dyspraxia struggled with handwriting and bilateral coordination tasks.
terms.contextNotes: Often co-occurs with ADHD and dyslexia.

terms.definition: A condition in which the nervous system has difficulty receiving and responding to sensory information appropriately.
terms.exampleSentence: The child with sensory processing disorder showed sensory avoiding behaviours in noisy classrooms.
terms.contextNotes: Often addressed via Ayres Sensory Integration approaches.

terms.definition: A neurodevelopmental condition affecting social communication and behaviour, often with restricted interests and sensory differences.
terms.exampleSentence: Visual schedules supported the child with autism spectrum disorder during transitions.
terms.contextNotes: DSM-5 replaced Asperger's with the broader ASD term.

terms.definition: A reduced intellectual ability and difficulty with everyday activities arising before adulthood and lasting throughout life.
terms.exampleSentence: Easy-read materials were used for the patient with a moderate learning disability.
terms.contextNotes: UK terminology; equivalent to intellectual disability internationally.

terms.definition: A group of mental health conditions characterised by excessive fear, worry and behavioural disturbance.
terms.exampleSentence: Graded exposure was used to address community access avoidance in her anxiety disorder.
terms.contextNotes: Often co-occurs with depression.

terms.definition: A mood disorder marked by persistent low mood, anhedonia, fatigue and reduced engagement in occupations.
terms.exampleSentence: Activity scheduling was introduced to address withdrawal associated with her depression.
terms.contextNotes: OT often supports behavioural activation and routine building.

terms.definition: A severe mental illness with positive symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions and negative symptoms such as withdrawal.
terms.exampleSentence: Vocational rehabilitation was offered to the patient with stable schizophrenia.
terms.contextNotes: Negative symptoms often most disabling for occupational performance.

terms.definition: A mood disorder characterised by alternating episodes of mania or hypomania and depression.
terms.exampleSentence: Routine and sleep hygiene were emphasised to support relapse prevention in bipolar disorder.
terms.contextNotes: Sleep disruption is a common relapse indicator.

terms.definition: A trauma-related disorder featuring intrusive memories, avoidance, hyperarousal and negative mood after a traumatic event.
terms.exampleSentence: Grounding strategies were introduced for hyperarousal in his post-traumatic stress disorder.
terms.contextNotes: OT input includes sensory grounding and routine restoration.

terms.definition: An eating disorder characterised by restricted intake, low body weight, fear of weight gain and disturbed body image.
terms.exampleSentence: Mealtime support and meaningful occupation were addressed in her anorexia nervosa care plan.
terms.contextNotes: OT may co-lead supported mealtimes on inpatient units.

terms.definition: Trauma to the bones, tendons, nerves or soft tissues of the hand requiring rehabilitation to restore function.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the hand injury, oedema management and tendon gliding exercises were initiated.
terms.contextNotes: Early referral to hand therapy improves outcomes.

terms.definition: A chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis with symmetrical small-joint synovitis, deformity and systemic features.
terms.exampleSentence: Joint protection and energy conservation were taught to the patient with rheumatoid arthritis.
terms.contextNotes: Hand deformities include ulnar drift and swan-neck.

terms.definition: A degenerative joint disorder featuring cartilage loss, osteophytes, joint pain and reduced function.
terms.exampleSentence: A long-handled reacher was issued to the patient with hip osteoarthritis awaiting surgery.
terms.contextNotes: Most common joint disorder in older adults.

terms.definition: A chronic demyelinating disease of the central nervous system causing relapsing or progressive neurological deficits.
terms.exampleSentence: Fatigue management was central to her multiple sclerosis rehabilitation programme.
terms.contextNotes: Heat sensitivity (Uhthoff's phenomenon) affects ADL performance.

terms.definition: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder of upper and lower motor neurons causing weakness, atrophy and bulbar symptoms.
terms.exampleSentence: Early environmental control referral was made for the patient with motor neurone disease.
terms.contextNotes: OT focus on equipment provision ahead of decline.

terms.definition: Brain damage caused by external mechanical force, ranging from mild concussion to severe focal and diffuse injury.
terms.exampleSentence: Cognitive rehabilitation was initiated following his severe traumatic brain injury.
terms.contextNotes: Severity guided by GCS and duration of post-traumatic amnesia.

terms.definition: Damage to the spinal cord causing motor, sensory and autonomic dysfunction below the level of injury.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the C6 spinal cord injury, tenodesis grip training was initiated.
terms.contextNotes: Functional outcomes mapped against ASIA level.

terms.definition: Compression of the median nerve at the wrist, causing pain, numbness and weakness in the lateral hand.
terms.exampleSentence: A neutral wrist splint was provided overnight for carpal tunnel syndrome.
terms.contextNotes: Symptoms typically worse at night.

terms.definition: A non-progressive disorder of movement and posture due to a lesion of the developing brain, with varied presentations.
terms.exampleSentence: The child with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy used ankle-foot orthoses for ambulation.
terms.contextNotes: Function classified using the GMFCS levels I-V.

terms.definition: A fracture occurring after low-energy trauma in osteoporotic bone, commonly at the hip, wrist or vertebrae.
terms.exampleSentence: Following the fragility fracture of the wrist, joint protection education was provided.
terms.contextNotes: Triggers a fracture liaison service review.

terms.definition: Tissue damage from thermal, chemical, electrical or radiation exposure, classified by depth and percentage body area.
terms.exampleSentence: Pressure garments and silicone gel were prescribed for hypertrophic scarring after the burn injury.
terms.contextNotes: OT begins early to prevent contracture and scarring.

terms.definition: A chronic pain condition usually following trauma, with disproportionate pain, oedema, autonomic and trophic changes.
terms.exampleSentence: Mirror therapy was introduced for the patient with complex regional pain syndrome of the hand.
terms.contextNotes: Diagnosed using the Budapest criteria.

terms.definition: Adhesive capsulitis of the glenohumeral joint causing pain and progressive global restriction of shoulder movement.
terms.exampleSentence: Joint protection and adapted dressing techniques were introduced for her frozen shoulder.
terms.contextNotes: Commonly affects adults aged 40-60 with a self-limiting course.

terms.definition: A progressive respiratory condition with airflow obstruction, breathlessness and reduced exercise tolerance.
terms.exampleSentence: Energy conservation and breathing control were taught for her chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
terms.contextNotes: OT input often via pulmonary rehabilitation programmes.
